Science Wins

Every Science Wins story we're smiling about, freshest first.

⚡ The Planet

For the First Time Ever, the World Made More Electricity From Wind and Sun Than Gas

In April 2026, wind and solar produced 531 terawatt-hours of power worldwide — overtaking gas for the first time.

via Ember · 6h

🔬 Science Wins

A Short Course of Immunotherapy Left Bowel Cancer Patients Cancer-Free for Nearly Three Years

In a UK trial, a few weeks of treatment before surgery worked so well that some patients didn't need surgery at all.

via Science Daily · 9h

💊 Science Wins

Two New Drugs Are Giving Pancreatic Cancer Patients Something Rare: More Time

One experimental drug doubled the odds of being alive after a year; another let patients live three to four times longer without the disease progressing.

via NPR · 14h

🪶 Science Wins

From 27 Birds to More Than 500: The California Condor's Long Climb Back From the Brink

In 1987 every last condor was in captivity. Today over half of them fly free.

via Earth.com · 16h

🤖 Good AI

An AI System Is Helping Doctors Crack the Hardest Rare-Disease Cases — and Show Its Work

A new 'agentic' system diagnoses rare diseases with reasoning doctors can actually follow and check.

via Nature · 20h

🦅 The Planet

Back From the Brink: America's Bald Eagle Soars From 500 Pairs to 14,000

Once down to fewer than 500 breeding pairs, the national bird has rebounded to roughly 14,000.

via U.S. Dept. of the Interior · 22h

🧬 Science Wins

Doctors Built a One-of-a-Kind Gene Therapy for a Sick Baby — in Just Six Months

An infant with a rare metabolic disorder received a CRISPR treatment designed specifically for him, start to finish, in record time.

via Innovative Genomics Institute · 1d

🐋 Good AI

Scientists Just Put $17 Million Toward Teaching AI to Translate Animal Languages

The Earth Species Project is using machine learning to map the calls of whales, crows and zebras — and build the first 'dictionaries' of animal communication.

via National Geographic · 1d

How Cloudbreak works

We comb publishers across the web for the stories that make your day a little brighter, show you the headline and a quick summary, then send you straight to the original source for the full story. We don't republish anyone's work — good news belongs to everyone, and credit belongs to the people who reported it.

Spotted something good?

A neighbor, a rescue, a small win on your street — if it made you smile, send it our way.

Send us a tip →
